skill.name: data-processor
skill.description: Process and transform arrays of data with common operations like filtering, mapping, and aggregation
skill.summary: Process and transform arrays of data with common operations like filtering, mapping, and aggregation
skill.author: ArtemisAI

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported shell execution, weak cryptography, and a hardcoded URL, but those specific matches are false positives in Markdown examples and prose. A real medium-risk concern remains because the documented processor executes caller-supplied JavaScript callbacks and includes MCP database and storage examples that require trusted inputs and permission review.

medium_findings.title: Caller-Supplied JavaScript Callback Execution
medium_findings.description: The documented processor executes functions provided in operations.filter and operations.map. This is useful for trusted local transformations, but unsafe if operation definitions come from untrusted users or external data.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code clearly invokes caller-provided callbacks with result.filter and result.map. Risk depends on whether callers restrict those functions to trusted code.

medium_findings.title: MCP Data Access Example Requires Permission Review
medium_findings.description: The integration example queries a database MCP tool and saves processed records through a storage MCP tool. This is not malicious, but users should review data scope and tool permissions before running it.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The MCP calls are visible in the example and could touch sensitive data. There is no evidence of unauthorized external transfer or hidden endpoint.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Markdown Backticks Not Shell Execution
low_findings.description: The static shell findings are Markdown code fences, inline code markers, or a JavaScript template literal. I found no Ruby backtick shell execution on these lines.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line review confirms these are documentation delimiters, inline code formatting, or JavaScript syntax. There is no shell interpreter or command execution operator.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptography Pattern Not Present
low_findings.description: The reported weak cryptography lines contain prose, YAML metadata, or sort order text. I found no MD5, SHA1, DES, RC4, or cryptographic API use.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line contents do not include cryptographic algorithms or hashing calls. The matches appear to come from unrelated substrings such as Markdown text and 'desc'.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Documentation URL Not Network Request
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URL is a documentation citation to an Anthropic article. It is not used by fetch, curl, a request library, or any runtime network operation.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URL appears only in Markdown reference text. No network API call or data transfer logic is present around it.

dangerous_patterns.title: Caller-Supplied JavaScript Callback Execution
dangerous_patterns.description: operations.filter and operations.map are invoked as functions over input records. This pattern should be limited to trusted callbacks and should not deserialize functions from untrusted input.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The function invocation pattern is explicit in the implementation example. The risk is contextual because the skill is documentation rather than a packaged service.

content.value_statement: Teams often need repeatable data cleanup without sending large records through prompts. This skill provides reusable JavaScript patterns for filtering, mapping, sorting, deduplication, and aggregation.
actual_capabilities: Filters arrays of objects with caller-defined conditions.
Maps records into normalized fields and values.
Sorts records by a selected field in ascending or descending order.
Calculates sum, average, count, minimum, and maximum for one field.
Removes duplicate records by a selected field.
Combines processing with MCP database and storage examples.
limitations: It is documented as Markdown and does not include a separate packaged JavaScript file.
Caller-supplied callbacks must come from trusted code.
Aggregation examples cover one field at a time.
It does not validate schemas or repair malformed records automatically.

use_cases.title: Clean Sales Exports
use_cases.description: Filter completed transactions, sort by amount, and summarize totals before sharing a compact report.

use_cases.title: Normalize Customer Lists
use_cases.description: Trim names, lowercase email addresses, remove duplicates, and keep only records that match business rules.

use_cases.title: Summarize MCP Query Results
use_cases.description: Process records returned from a database tool and store only the needed results for later use.

prompt_templates.title: Filter Records
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the data-processor skill to filter this array to records where status is active. Return the kept count and removed count.

prompt_templates.title: Normalize Fields
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the data-processor skill to trim names, lowercase emails, and keep the original id field. Return a short summary.

prompt_templates.title: Aggregate Revenue
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the data-processor skill to filter completed orders, sort by total descending, and calculate the total revenue.

prompt_templates.title: Process MCP Results
prompt_templates.prompt: Query approved customer records with the MCP database tool, process them with data-processor, save the reduced output, and return only summary statistics.

output_examples.input: A sales list with completed and pending orders.
output_examples.output: The result keeps completed orders, sorts them by amount from high to low, and reports one removed record.

output_examples.input: A customer list with duplicate emails and inconsistent capitalization.
output_examples.output: The result contains normalized names and emails, removes duplicates, and reports the final customer count.

output_examples.input: A set of order totals that need a revenue summary.
output_examples.output: The result reports the selected aggregate field, the sum operation, and the calculated total value.

best_practices: Validate input arrays before running transformations.
Use trusted callback functions and review MCP tool permissions.
Return summary statistics instead of large processed datasets.
anti_patterns: Do not execute callbacks generated from untrusted user text.
Do not process sensitive records without checking storage and database permissions.
Do not rely on this skill for schema validation or data repair.

faq.question: What data formats does this skill process?
faq.answer: It is designed for arrays of JavaScript objects.

faq.question: Does the skill include a runnable package file?
faq.answer: No. The repository provides the implementation as Markdown example code.

faq.question: Can it handle large datasets?
faq.answer: The documentation describes efficient array methods and example performance for up to 100,000 records.

faq.question: Can I use it with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code?
faq.answer: Yes. The report marks support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: Is it safe for untrusted transformation rules?
faq.answer: No. Only run filter and map callbacks that come from trusted code.

faq.question: Does it make network requests?
faq.answer: No direct runtime request is shown. The only URL is a documentation link.
